body.notes: Participation in IETF meetings constitutes agreement to the Note Well, which permits recording, transcription, and public archival of proceedings. See also BCP 78 (RFC 5378) and BCP 79 (RFC 8179) for IPR policies.

body.title: Ad Hoc On-demand Distance Vector Version 2 (AODVv2) Routing
Transmission of IP Packets over Overlay Multilink Network (OMNI) Interfaces
IPv6 Addresses for Ad Hoc Networks

transcript.text: Hi, Jim. >> Hi, Jim. >> Well, it's time. I've got 12:00. >> Close the door. >> Yeah. All right. Welcome to from Madrid uh and the mobile ad hoc session. Um, you've probably all seen this, but I just want to uh bring your attention to the note. Well, um, by participating in the IATF, you agree to follow the IATF uh, process and policies and there's a a number of best current practices uh, that you should be familiar with that are outlined on this page. And we want you to know really well um that the ITF meetings and the virtual meetings are for professional collaboration. Um we want to create a an environment and in which pe um many people can >> different backgrounds >> different participate in um and we don't tolerate uh any har uh harassment uh while at the ITF meetings or virtual meetings or social events and if you have any issues uh there are um you're encouraged to bring them up with the uh ombbits person. So, uh meeting uh points um and and tips just to make the meeting go are outlined here. Please remember to sign in if you're in the local locally in the room. And uh for remote remote participants, um please make sure your mics are off when you're not uh um participating. And just here's a a reference for the resources for these meetings. and the resources for the workroom session, the agenda today. Um, so we're going to go through um uh the chair's uh the the draft status and then we have an update on uh Kira performance by Roland Bless. Uh we have an update on some implementation on AODVD2 from Charlie Perkins and then um we have a presentation on Manet interworking from Fred Templan and then we're going to discuss about uh the charter and um the uh the issues uh that we uh currently face with participation etc. at at the end here and then there'll be uh time for wrap up. Anything else you want? >> Oh yes. Um if we could get a notetaker um uh the session is being recorded and and stuff but it it would help if uh anybody wants to contribute to the notes and uh yeah sorry I'm having trouble getting that uh stupid thing to get me in. So I I am here but >> can I make a request that we move the charter discussion to to I may have to step out. >> Okay. >> Um so can we do that first before we do the presentations if nobody objects and have that conversation because obviously I need to be here for that but I may have to I may not have to step out but I may have to. So I don't want to miss that obviously. >> All right. Um okay. You want to you want to take that and leave with that? >> Uh Yes. Um, as you can already see on the agenda, uh, first it said, uh, new charter discussion and that was on the agenda as posted last week. However, in the meantime, we, uh, talked to our ad and, um, he has, uh, uh, some maybe wellfounded objections to his rechartering at this stage. uh given the level of energy uh in the group uh as he sees it. Um um so at this point in time uh we are not going to recharter. We keep going uh under the current charter. Um and the discussion that we now need to have is what we can and cannot do under the current charter. Um so uh maybe Don if you can uh advance the slides to the point where we have the current charter. Ah yes. So this this was uh copied verbatim from what's uh on the uh on the Monet uh list and uh I think the first part is more or less uh boilerplate uh that you may have seen before. So I would go to the next one. Um there the first paragraph covers DAB and DAB extensions that work has been done. Uh some extensions are still in the pipeline. Um we are also responsible for DAP maintenance. So uh new uh new DP extensions may be coming our way. Um so so that's all good. Um and then there's a bit about coordination with other groups. Um Babel has now been uh integrated into Luminina working group so to speak. Um so we are yeah although it doesn't say that in the charter yet we are uh also uh doing uh Babel maintenance and extensions and that's why uh Donald is added as a third chair to this group. Um, Donald being the former chair of Babel. [Music] Um, we are obviously uh still doing the uh maintenance of uh standard track uh protocols that have been developed in this group before. Um yeah uh you see there the work items as the group saw them back in 2016 when this uh the current charter was uh established. Uh some of these things have been done some of them have been uh overcome by events I would say. Um we have developed a a dynamic link exchange protocol. We did do a credit window scheme. Um um we never did the statistics. We never did the uh multiccast forwarding information base for manet. Um and we did not document the challenges best practices for deploying and managing mana networks because uh the feeling was that um there's such a large uh difference in practices uh out there for for people who deploy manise and they're not always willing forthcoming with with the details about their setups that this would be a a very uh difficult effort. So the next slide and uh the seven slides thereafter uh have had the proposed text uh for a new new charter. And this text was also uh posted to the mailing list earlier this week by uh by Donald. But uh we can consider this now as overcome by events and for reference only. But um if we get to the discussions of what we can do under the current charter uh maybe it's good to look at some of these things. And if we advance a bit further, uh, Don, can we [Music] Okay, this is still old text that is has been retained. Um, this is about DAB. [Music] Go on further. Um, this is uh something new. Um this was uh brought in um by people from uh Frown over FKIE in Germany who have already uh contributed in the past to the working group. Um most recently with uh a number of DAP extension drafts that are still in the pipeline. Um and they have some ideas uh on um having a rest interface and a young model on uh a manet router that um allows external parties like uh uh nodes running applications or uh network management stations to uh retrieve uh the state of the network as seen by that router and that information can come from DAB and it can come from uh the routing protocols that are running on that router and this uh aggregated information would be uh made available through this uh res interface and this is work that I think they have already begun to do there's not an internet draft for this yet but uh I feel that uh we could have that uh before uh say a next meeting. Uh and at least there is uh as I sense it a lot of commitment to work on this and that is something that has been lacking sometimes on other uh uh things that we had listed as as work items. So a question would be would something like this uh can something like this be made to fit under the current charter? [Music] Um now you've seen the charter text so I would like to hear opinions. Um my my personal opinion head off is uh that well there is something about uh about network management in there but that's different that's about doing anformational draft and this is uh as far as I can see it uh aiming at the standard stric [Music] Do we want to address item by item or do we want to just keep going, Jim? >> Oh. Oh. So, >> okay. >> Okay. >> I I would prefer to do item by item because otherwise we get confused. Okay, Lou Ber. Uh, yeah, I took your statement as you want to discuss it now. If you want to discuss it later, I'm I'm I'm fine to wait. >> Always fine. >> Okay. So, for me, um, uh, exposing things via uh, Yang uh, is very much in the spirit of what has been in the charter. Uh, I know when I started looking at DAP, I said, why aren't we using a Yang model for this? And the answer was well history. So if history brought us to Dup and at that time we thought about using Yang it seems very reasonable to say okay deops the past now let's also look at using Yang today for some the the superset of the information. It it to me it seems within the spirit of the charter although I don't believe it's in the language of the charter. So but I I don't see that as a a big change. So that's answering your specific question. I actually had a uh I um I have a comment on the language but I see an ad coming up so I don't know if he wants to respond to the first point before I go to the technical point. >> Sorry I didn't mean to interrupt. So so this this is my personal view. So I need to look at it in a bit more detail. But um you know if I look at all the other working groups I have everybody does a Yang model for basically everything. And most of the charters don't specifically call that out as a as a work item. So I I I think that I'd be comfortable with being able to do you know this yang stuff and I I don't think I would have a problem. But so we can look at it. So that would be my preference as we talked before is like if we can if there's work that want that we can do now that is in the charter or can be fitted into the existing charter then that's great. What I what I don't want to do is recharter the working group for documents where there's just one person that's interested in the thing and is only one person who's going to work on it. Um, and I don't want to recharter for work that has already been socialized elsewhere within the ITF and so far not got the community behind it. So that's why I'm reluctant to do a recharter. But if if we you know I'm I I I would also say I'm not in any hurry to close the working group. So if there is genuine work that fits within the charter, then let's do that. um is kind of where I where I fit and and this I think fits in the charter from basically what you said. So I think we're good. >> Great. Thank you. The uh um the specific point I wanted to raise on the language here is why are we talking about rest comp and JSON? When we do a Yang model, the power of the Yang model is that it's independent of encoding and it's independent of transport. Why are we talking about specific encoding and transport here? >> Good question. Um I don't have a good answer for that, but I will. >> Okay. So I think I I think you know I certainly would fully get behind the ANG work and could see the value in that. Uh if there is a reason that in a specific operational environment it makes sense to use one transport over the other that might be a good you know applicability statement but it's not tied into the basic model definition. So I think there are two separate points here. Um, one of them I'm fully convinced on. The other one I'm interested in in hearing why. >> Okay. Noted. So you're saying that uh whether you make this uh young model accessible via rest interface is an implementational thing. So whether you use let's say rest comp or core comp is a sort of a environment constraint an operational environment constraint or decision and there may be good reason to use restcom for man a for it to be recommended but that's a separate conversation from defining the model for the information you want to make available so I can see the yang model and it makes a lot of sense to me or Yang models. I don't understand the motivation for calling out rest comp and JSON and I'd like to understand that. I'm not saying it's a bad idea. I'm not saying it's a good idea. I'm just saying I don't understand but it's and that's one and two is it's completely separate. >> Yes, it's clear. Um what yeah one one comment from my side about uh DAB and uh and this young model. So so this is uh intended to get the information from the router uh and not directly from the modems. So the first the router will aggregate the information then make it available to the outside world. Let's move on. Okay. Um, this is going to be more contentious then I think. Um, I looked at the charter before the current charter and then there were that was very nice and and short and there were basically two main items uh which was dual standard tracks proactive protocol which became OS RV2 and do a standard track reactive protocol which uh went under different names started as do and then became name AODV2. Um and that uh for various reasons never materialized uh until the uh chairs and ad uh at the time in 2016 uh decided to recharter and and gave us the charter that we currently are under and there was no longer uh a work item for uh developing a reactive protocol. And I believe that AODV V2 continued for a while as a um AD sponsored uh document, but uh Charlie would know more about this. Uh and now there's the idea to bring it back. Um um and personally again no head I would like to see that happen because I think I have a use case for this and uh I also to the best of my abilities would be uh willing to contribute. Um but uh I don't immediately see this fitting in the charter. So I wonder whether someone has any comments on that. >> Yeah, I I looked at this and you said that the um original AODB draft is experimental. So this work as follow on but trying to be standardsbased is that outside of the charter I guess is the question, right? And I I think there's still another question is is there enough people to work on it too at this time point in time? >> Yeah. So Jim um so my read of the charter is it's not in charter um because it was specifically taken out of charter um in uh as you say what 2016 or something. So in order for you to progress that to standards track, number one, it needs to be in charter, which means we need to do a recharter to do that. And in order for me to be convinced to do a recharter, I need to see working group consensus that there are enough people that want this work and are prepared to work on it. So that's that's the bottom line. So um if there's if if the working group and it it has that consensus then okay we'll have to go through the recharter process to be able to do it but that's not the sense that I've gotten um but I'm open to being wrong that's clear so the uh the onus uh is on the working group to uh show that uh people are working on this um Okay, go ahead, Charlie. >> Charlie, we can't hear you. If you're >> Am I in the queue to say something? >> Yes. >> Okay. Well, uh perhaps best would be for me to um you know take any questions during my presentation, but uh uh I don't know exactly how much participation is needed. There obviously has been some participation in the working group and uh at the time when this uh protocol work was taken out of the charter it was um well it wasn't really a smooth working process. I'll just say it that way. And then um AODV I for me personally uh it was yes indeed it was a personal draft uh for a while but I was involved with a lot of other things and it didn't um didn't really push on me to finish it. But at the time when the draft was taken out of the working group charter, it was already practically done and there had been a lot of reviews of the document and um many changes required for uh conforming not only to RSC 5444 but also the language of um OSRV2 draft. So, so anyway, uh I will I guess I'll just leave it. I wanted to respond to Jen's point uh directly and we'll just leave everything else for if it comes up during my presentation. Thank you. >> Lou, um you wrote something in the chat. Uh, is that for AODP? I heard um Jim suggest that he'd like to see support. So, I'm offering maybe you want to do a show of hands poll to at least get the temperature of the room. Alternatively, you just do it on list. Eventually, you're going to have to do it on list either way. Okay. Can we do this? >> Show hands. >> Yum. Are you interested in working? Don't be concerned. I didn't >> It's still up for show. All right. Seems that. All right. Is that sufficient? I think we've given the size of the group time. That's it. So now I'll terminate. >> All right. Next thing. >> Yeah. Yeah. You need to Yeah, all of us we have no time for presentations. Um yeah, this is basically um prompted by um the work that uh Fred proposed but it is not the work itself because in the discussion among the chairs uh we came to the conclusion that the first thing that would need would be needed was a gap analysis. Is there really a problem here? Is there something that needs to be done? Um, and even then, uh, later we could, if that turns out to be the case, then we get to the obvious next question, who's willing to work on it? But the first thing would be to do this gap analysis. Um my um take on the current charter is that this gap analysis at least could be done under uh the management uh part of the current charter. Jim, if you want to speak anytime. So just to be clear, you can do a gap analysis. You don't have to publish a document as an RFC. There's no reason why you can't do anformational document that's doing this gap analysis and and that's fine as long you know the charter is important if you want that work to go through to RFC. If you don't, you can use it as input to make a decision as a working group um on whether you want to do that work or not. This is explicitly in charter. Yeah. Read your deliverables. >> I actually did >> the presentations. >> Uh in the interest of time and giving the presenters time, we should move to the presentations. >> There's only two more spots. stuff that's already covered >> then quickly quickly click through them uh and then bring up the first presentation. So we can we can close off on this topic but you know from what I heard um there's some work that can probably be covered in under the existing charter and you can focus on that and then um based upon that gap analysis that you do may generate work that would require us to rechart it. Um that's not taken into account the AODV uh V2 stuff. Um, the chairs need to make a determination on that that I mean, you've got the poll, but presumably you'll probably you're going to need to go to the mailing list to to actually find out who's actually willing to do this. Um, so that's kind of where we're at. So, so I guess it's my summary would be that, you know, there's not an immediate recharter going to happen. There's some work that needs to happen first. Um and the output of that will determine whether there's a recharter or not. >> Okay, that's clear. Thank you. >> Yeah, and thank you for taking it first. So, because I I I am going to need to step out because I I've just got another thing. So, um so thank you for doing that. Appreciate it. >> No problem. >> And uh you're up, Roland. Um I just cut the time down a little bit so that I can we can get all the presentations. >> Yeah, sure. So try to stick to >> Hi, I'm I'm Roland. Just want to give a brief update on on Cira uh in Monets. Um yeah, so Cura started out as routing protocol for control planes being zero touch scalable and so on. So the idea was or so there were comments from the community why not how does it perform in mobile ad hoc networks? Um so the features are basically it's an ID based protocol um tries to be efficient by using um proximity neighbor selection proximity routing. It has things like path discovery. So it will take a first path that is using mainly the overlay routing and then their answer comes back you have a later path. It's a little bit between proactive and reactive protocols. um has dynamics and stuff. Uh it is loop free even during conversions and so on. And a nice additional thing is that it also provides a key value store. Uh so you can basically um register names and services and look up uh do do the lookup. Um so notes can actually find uh each other and um that could also be used for example to detect whether there are any uh hybrid nodes that that have uh actually access to the internet. Um what recently has been done is um since the last presentation I I had in Dublin was um improving on uh some things like path validation. So nodes are trusting more or less the information that are in the source routes which uh Kira uses um but uh routing information from other nodes must be validated before they are actually used. Um and also we came up with a scheme that we called fast vicinity alternatives. Since Kira maintains um full two hop vicinity graph, it can pre or to premputee the path ids it uses in its forwarding tier. Um we can use that in order to to quickly find alternative path in case a link fails. So for example in the the lower part here if the direct link fails uh then maybe if there is another path to that node then can uh easily find that and and use that. Um we've done that modification and you can see in the comparison of the the old version and the new version that this already improves a bit. um the delivery ratio which is shown here. Very simple scenario here and also the stretch values got a little bit better. Um yeah because the the routes are then uh little bit more efficient than using the ordinary regular rediscovery mechanism. Uh another thing that is not yet document documented in the internet draft is a fast nexttop detour. Um that is basically the case if um let's say in the example here if the data packet is uh sent um along a link to the next node and it should follow basically the the next stop from B to C then this link may have been a problem and then in in this case um the data packet actually um can be um redirected via some alternative path. Um meanwhile the control plane will inform the node that the link is broken so that it can update its its uh invalid routes and um the nice thing about that it works in the forwarding tier. So the the detour um can be simply uh directly be used in case the noticees that the direct link to see um has has been failing. So in that case it simply puts another path ID on top of the data packet and this gets removed at the next node which would be X and then the packet arrives at C and the normal forwarding can continue. So that is without any modification of the the current scheme. We that the cost is that we add another path ID on top. Um we've done a little bit more simulations. So this is a larger scenario where you have uh 600 nodes roughly also random waypoint model the usual Wi-Fi range and you can see that that kira actually doesn't perform so bad in the sense that you have um quite good uh delivery ratio for data packets which is above 9 95% and uh control messages even higher due to the source routing that we are using which is more robust due to uh with respect to the changes and yeah then also packet stretch is quite acceptable and um overhead could be possibly improved um there's no real manet specific tuning right now so one could work on that and we did some not direct comparison to other protocols so we did not implement the other protocols but we looked at the scenarios from the literature use the same scenario and we can see here in comparison to some other protocols that the delivery ratio is is also quite nice in comparison to existing money protocols. Um yeah, if you're interested in that work, I would really like to hear your comments. As I said, it's not meant to be a money protocol, but it can actually also work quite well in that um environment. and what we are doing with our existing implementation uh to perform some tests in a in a real Wi-Fi mesh. So yeah, if you're interested, please comment on the list or also on the routing error working group mailing because that that would be nice. Thanks. >> Thank you. Uh we'll move on to the next presentation because we're a little tight on time and at the end we'll do the document status that we missed or skipped over the slide. Can you do it? >> Yeah. Okay. Does anybody know how to switch the slides out? I lost control. Okay, I think I got it. Sorry. Next one. All right. Sorry, Charlie. You're up. Okay. Can Can you hear me? >> Yep. >> Okay. And let's see now if there's a on this screen. I do not see something that says slide clicker. Oh, do you want Yeah. Do you want to control the slides? So, sorry. >> Well, it's probably easier, but you can if you want. >> You've got control. >> Okay. Um anyway, well good morning. It's very early here uh on Charles Perkins and um I u last year was um it was suggested that IODV version two work could be revived and so we um um decided to go ahead and uh made a updated the uh document as as needed and with ID nets and so on there wasn't too many uh changes involved to the actual text. Um but then on the list there was a a lot of discussion about implementation. So that is why I made this presentation about the implementations that I was aware of. Um it's currently an individual draft as was mentioned. It had been an AD sponsored draft for a while. Um, Alvaro uh was AD and was sponsoring it at the time. Um, I was u employed doing other things and also at the there was a AODB ripple draft that uh um looked and that was interesting work and it has been um it's in the RFC editor's queue. It's basically done. So, uh, this new draft for AODB version two, um, is there and, uh, ready to, if you're interested to take a look. Uh the original work as mentioned was um it's a experimental protocol that was to combine the all the work in the working group at that time on reactive protocols which basically ended up being um dynamic source routing DSR and AODB ad hoc ondemand distance vector routing. So that went on for a while and in a sense there was two protocols um he and Shakuras who was also co-chair at that time uh decided we should call it Daimo to not favor either one of the protocols and that went on for a while and then they um eventually the folks in DSR uh didn't really help us very much and they were not participy ipating and not only that but um we did some work and it showed that the path accumulation function of DSR in many situations uh was detrimental to performance basically because it made the package larger. I I should mention that a lot of interest in AODV and AODD version two had to do with scalability and I didn't bring the results but it's published we we simulated up to 10,000 nodes and the scalability of reactor protocols is really very good even up to such huge um populations. So some of the um um what we learned from that work um as I mentioned that the the path accumulation function was it was ambivalent. It helps sometime and hurts sometime and it has other features as well that you might learn about if you're interested. Um so um so anyway um as also mentioned because of um relatively um unhappy situation AODV was taken out of the charter and then it could be put back in and at the time it was removed it was practically done uh at least in my opinion And uh as far as uh technical issues in the draft, I'm not aware of anything that needs to be um that I'm not aware of technical problems with the draft. >> Uh even uh even the um uh the security section was pretty well uh combed through and and found to be okay. >> Anyway, this is about the implementations. Uh I I listed these implementations here as you can see uh their simulations and um uh protocol design. Was there a question? Did I miss a question? >> I just was I was just going to be mindful of the time. I saw you had 12 slides and you were talking and I just want to make sure time. >> Thank you. >> Okay. Yes, I will I will hurry up. Uh so these are all published and I won't go into I won't the point of the rest of the presentation was uh to say some things about each one of these and I will now do that but uh this uh obviously you can go get the papers if you want. I I have the papers by the way also if you're interested but I don't prefer to give them out without permission of the authors. um AODV was uh uh analyzed this this paper is about uh 12 years old. So this was when the protocol was actually a work item in the in the Manet group. So they did simulations under NS2 and um their results in a nutshell was that the AODV version two performance was uniformly better than AODV. And by the way, this paper does not bring it out, but at least in our work, uh, we decided that to take out the hello function because it was really just causing a lot of collisions and almost always had no beneficial effect. So, uh, especially on layers two where the layer 2 link between nodes are more reliable. So, anyway, uh, if you take out hello, you get some good results. That's what we found. Um this uh I'm also included lot of steambrink she's a co-author of AODB version two and she had her master thesis and did an implementation uh but I don't ever remember getting um u information about her results on implementation so I can try to get that as well uh this protocol was simulated under Omni which is a well-known simulating system. And um again uh this is for AODV versus DAO versus AODV2 which basically means AODD version two against a previous revision of DAO. And I think this previous revision of DAO is one that had the path accumulation function. So in a nutshell, AODV version two uh in this uh simulation test bed uh really is work works better at least on these performance metrics. um this paper and uh I found it by looking at looking through the um um bibliography for some of the papers and it's interesting because it was targeted for a Raspberry Pi and they were planning to use location information as well which has been published in other areas for AODB uh using location information and it has obviously benefits and um uh maybe some occasional problems as well. So I >> Charlie can we one more presentation and >> we're running. >> Okay. Well, you know, everyone is like that. Uh we have um these publications and we have used for space implementation and a relatively um complete design. Uh the recent work was an NS3. It also shows improvements in results compared to AODV. Uh and that's that's uh you can look at the slides later and get an idea about the counter performance. Uh lastly, I'll just uh wrap up to say that uh I am interested in um doing the work and and finishing it. Um what I what these simulations and uh discussions does not reiterate that the scalability of AODB and AODB version two is really really extremely good for large ad hoc networks. And I guess I'm more than out of time. Thank you for your your uh interest and I'll uh be available if needed. Thank you, Fred. We're we're ready. I'll give you >> Do you have me now? >> Yeah. >> Okay. All right. This presentation is about man inter networking. Um I got control so I'm going to go to the next slide. >> You've got a control if you want. >> Okay. So, uh mobile ad hoc networks are very interesting for connecting up devices that need to do devicetodevice communications in a multihop kind of a fashion. But where they really become interesting is when you hook them up to the internet. And here we have a slide showing two different min connected to the internet by a a proxy node that's shown in green here. Then 5G space that might be the G node B for example. So within each mana we can do multihop routing thanks to the mana routing protocols like OSRv2 and uh uh and and and Babel and and possibly even AOBV to V2 as as well. So within each mana we can already do that with using the mana routing protocols. We can do multi hopping from node to node device to device. But where it becomes interesting is how can we get a node that's within the mana on the left hand side to connect to a correspondent node in the internet. And for that case a nomatic address for that node is fine because the node is calling out from the mana out to a a fixed node in the internet where the address of the node in the internet is is is pervasively available. But on the right hand side we show a correspondent node in the internet calling into a node in a in a mobile ad hoc network. And what that means is that that mana node requires a stable address that doesn't change even if it moves around. So for example, if that mana router was to move from mana 2 to manet one, it should still be addressable using the same stable IP address or subnet prefix. And then another interesting case comes when you using the internet as transit between two different manas. So a node in in a first mana calling out through the internet as transit then into a second mana. And in that case both of those nodes both those mana nodes require a stable address that doesn't change even as nodes move about throughout the throughout the network. And the way that that we we can make that happen is by establishing an overlay over the mayonnaise in the internet. Now does this mean that we need to have one big overlay for the entire internet? No, it doesn't mean that. What it means is that a mobility service provider provides the overlay that nodes can join and there can be many different mo uh mobility service providers in in in the internet each with their own overlays. So, I'm going to turn down some to some mailing list posts that were put on the list over the course of the last couple weeks. Uh, so, uh, this was from 717 2025. Uh, when configuring an overlay multi-link network interface on each man router, we can use standard and unmodified IPv6 auto configuration protocols such as IP version 6 neighbor discovery and DHCPV6. uh it would work the same way for any manual router protocol and uh I'm already doing this today using an unmodified KIA DHCPv6 server in the RAVVD uh IPv6 neighbor discovery implementation. Those are are currently working in the the code that I've got for for doing the the overlay. And then a post that was on 718 2025. Um remember we said that man routing within the context of this working group assumes standard IP forwarding rules including the hop by hop decing to the the hop limit and TTL. So if you want to treat the man as a single unified link you need to configure an overlay which is exactly what the arrow omni service does. Uh it's based on IP version 6 encapsulation as an adaptation layer service and again sta standard services like DHCP version 6 and IP version 6 neighbor discovery work as normal. Uh the overlay approach also supports heterogeneous radio types and multiple interfaces per node in the lower layers. Uh but what it's really good for is mobile global mobile internet networking service for all airline and cap space mobility use cases. And that's the last of my charts. So I just wanted to call out those points that were already discussed in the list and highlight some of the uh the important points of those messages. I'll take any questions that there might be. So I guess the question um Jim had brought up the point about um we had the item on the the last item we spoke on on the on the charter that uh was related to the the problem and um and uh you know like outlining the problem not necessarily a solution with which you're proposing both but um it it would be helpful I think if you could help um with the uh the problem definition here and you know I I think that would be valuable uh contribution to the work group that's within the the charter right now. >> I can I can help with that. I think some of the earlier charts kind of hindered us with some of the problems we could discuss in a document like that. >> Anybody else? >> Yes. And uh since we're almost out of time uh I would encourage everybody to continue this discussion on the list uh because uh yeah I think that would be useful and uh would also inform this uh gap analysis that we uh are going to do. So, was there anything else you wanted to cover on the document status? We skipped over this part. >> Um, yeah, people can, uh, read it uh on the J slice. So, so credit based flow control is done. The the drafts are in the RCS quue hopefully to come out not uh in not too much time from now. Um then we have um the deal extensions from Hanninga from Brown over uh these have been in the have been adopted uh as working group uh documents quite some time ago. Uh I uh have seen some discussion uh more is always better. But uh maybe the way to bring out the discussion is to working group must call them uh somewhere u say within a month from now and um and then we'll see uh what happens and uh if if they seem uh u mature enough then we will uh send them on their way out of the group. Yeah, they're they're fairly small and compact drafts. Um uh the and I'm I've looked over them and uh I'm with the with the regards to being a shepherd for them. I know there's some uh rooting area uh early early review and um I'm going to make sure that those comments have have been addressed. And then also the only other thing is um they they have uh values and um uh there's a number of values like frequency etc that have been proposed. It would be good to get feedback that those are the right numbers and the right um scale of numbers. And then there's one more slide on individual drafts and uh I want to apologize to uh Christopher Dof again for not uh getting around to uh reviewing his draft. If I do review it, I probably have to revisit uh RC7181 quite heavily to uh to refresh my memory on that. Um I will also try to persuade Hanning Roger to have a look at this because he's much more knowledgeable on all matters related to OSR V2 than I am. But uh yeah, I I would like to have done it and I have to apologize again. >> Okay. And with that, I think we're we're done. And uh unless there's any other questions, I'd like to thank you on behalf of the chairs and we're having a quick session here. Thank you. It wasn't a quick session. It was a mon. I stand correct. >> Thank you all.
